title: Sensitivity Analysis of the Cost Coefficients in Multiobjective Integer Linear Optimization
abstract: This paper considers sensitivity analysis of the cost coefficients in multiobjective integer linear programming problems. We define the sensitivity region as the set of simultaneous changes to the coefficients for which the efficient set and its structure remain the same. In particular, we require that the component-wise relation between efficient solutions is preserved and that inefficient solutions remain inefficient, and we show that this is sufficient for the efficient set to be the same upon changes. For a single coefficient, we show that a subset of the inefficient solutions can be excluded from consideration. More specifically, we prove that it suffices to inspect the inefficient solutions of aq-objective problem that are efficient in one of two relatedq+вҖү1-objective problems. Finally, we show that the sensitivity region is a convex set (an interval). Our approach generalizes to simultaneous changes in multiple coefficients. For illustration, we consider mean-variance capital budgeting and determine the region for which the set of efficient portfolios remains the same, despite a misspecification or a change in the net present values of the projects. Further computational experience with multiobjective binary and integer knapsack problems demonstrates the general applicability of our technique. For instance, we obtain all sensitivity intervals for changes to single coefficients of biobjective problems with 500 binary variables in less than half an hour of CPU time by excluding a large number of inefficient solutions. In fact, the number of excluded solutions is above 100 orders of magnitude larger than the number of remaining solutions.

article.1. Introduction: For any type of optimization problems, sensitivity analysis is an integral part of modeling and decision making. It provides insights to the decision maker regarding the stability of solutions and thereby contributes to the confidence of managers and analysts in making decisions. As such, sensitivity analysis can assist companies in uncovering the impacts of inaccurate measurement or insufficient knowledge of the data, continuously changing market conditions and unexpected events. Its managerial importance is evidenced by the amount of literature in single-objective optimization. Early examples areLabbГ© et al. (1991), who consider sensitivity of the cost coefficients in continuous and discrete facility location problems;Glasserman and Tayur (1995), analyzing a production inventory system with respect to policy parameters; andRuben and Jacobs (1999), who perform sensitivity analysis on workforce level and batch size for a walk-and-pick method of order retrieval in a warehouse. As a more recent example,Topaloglu and Powell (2007)study sensitivity of stochastic dynamic fleet management with respect to fleet size and load availability. For practical approaches to global sensitivity analysis in constrained optimization, including linear programming (LP) models and small-scale combinatorial models, seeWagner (1995), and for a recent introduction to sensitivity analysis for management scientists, seeBorgonova (2017). In many real-world optimization problems, a decision maker considers several conflicting objectives. The purpose of multiobjective programming is to provide support for such decision making. In formalizing the problem, the decision maker has to define the relevant objectives and constraints, obtain parameter values, and deal with uncertainties; seeKeeney and Raiffa (1976)andSteuer (1986). A famous example of biobjective optimization is the mean-variance portfolio problem for capital budgeting. One objective is to maximize the expected net present value of a number of projects, whereas the other objective is to minimize risk, measured by the variance. In the discrete version of the problem, projects are either accepted or rejected. With independence between the projects, the problem can be formulated as an integer linear program, cf.Rosenblatt and Sinuany-Stern (1989). If the project cash flows cannot be specified with certainty or the problem has to be solved repeatedly under changes in the economic environment, sensitivity analysis is crucial. Other evident applications of biobjective programming arise in energy optimization, with conflicts between costs and environmental concerns. For instance,Norouzi et al. (2014)consider the short-term scheduling of hydro- and thermal electricity generation units, minimizing operating costs and carbon dioxide emissions from the thermal units. Similarly,Fazlollahi and MarГ©chal (2013)optimize investment and operation of energy technologies, including the adoption of biomass resources, such as to balance costs and emissions. For both problems, sensitivity analysis becomes highly relevant when cost estimates are uncertain and/or may vary over time. Likewise with a green perspective,Zarouk et al. (2022)address vehicle routing, balancing energy consumption and customer satisfaction. Because the latter involves penalties for violations of the time window constraints and for expected unsatisfied demand, the problem is triobjective. The difficulty of quantifying penalties justifies a thorough sensitivity analysis. A related application of multiobjective programming is the environmental investment problem ofHiggins et al. (2008), formulated as a pure integer programming problem. Another triobjective pure integer programming example includes the decision support methodology ofJohnson and Hurter (2000), developed to assist the design of housing mobility programs. The authors maximize economic benefits and minimize fairness disparities for community equity and employer equity, respectively. Like penalties, such fairness measures are hard to quantify. Motivated by these applications, the current paper considers a multiobjective integer linear program with objectives and constraints already defined, and aims to develop sensitivity analyses for the objective function coefficients. For single-objective optimization, sensitivity analysis determines whether a solution remains optimal upon a given change in the parameters or determines a range of parameters for which a solution remains optimal. Companies typically rely on decision-making tools such as standard software packages for optimization. For single-objective linear optimization, these packages1provide such sensitivity information. Sensitivity analysis for integer and multiobjective optimization, however, is a much less developed area of research and, with an absence of practical methods for computation, is not incorporated into standard software. 1.1. Literature Review In linear programming, sensitivity analysis computes the region of changes to the parameters for which the optimal basis remains the same; seeZionts (1974). In mixed-integer linear programming (MILP), sensitivity analysis comes down to sensitivity analysis for the convex hull of feasible solutions. The purpose is likewise to compute the region of changes for which the optimal solution is unchanged, cf.Jenkins (1990). Nevertheless, the lack of an explicit description of the convex hull makes this much more challenging. In this paper, we broaden the scope and consider sensitivity analysis of the objective function coefficients in multiobjective integer linear programming (MOILP). In (nontrivial) multiobjective programming, no single solution optimizes all objectives. Rather, there exists a set of efficient solutions that are incommensurable in the sense that if a solution has a better value than some other solution of one objective function, then it has a worse value of one of the other objective functions. As discussed in the following, a number of papers are devoted to sensitivity analysis of the objective function coefficients in multiobjective linear programming (MOLP). Previous developments, however, are largely restricted to MOLP and do not generalize to MOILP. Sensitivity analysis for LP was originally restricted to variations in a single coefficient. During the 1980s, however,Wendell (1984,1985) andRavi and Wendell (1985,1989) developed a tolerance approach for both independent and simultaneous changes of multiple coefficients. InHansen et al. (1989), the tolerance approach is extended to MOLP. The idea is to formulate a weighted sum problem and solve it to optimality for a space of varying weights on the objective functions, potentially constrained by the decision maker. An optimal solution to the weighted sum problem is also an efficient solution; seeGeoffrion (1968). The additive and the multiplicative tolerance approaches of this paper both focus on variations in the weights for which a specific optimal/efficient solution remains unchanged. In particular,Geoffrion (1968)derives the maximum percentage by which all weights may change. As a continuation of the work,MГЎrmol and Puerto (1997)demonstrate how to determine this maximum percentage with limited efforts.HladГӯc (2008), too, uses the weighted sum method and obtains percentage tolerances that preserve efficiency of a solution. Because these tolerances may not be maximal,HladГӯc and Sitarz (2013)proceed to establish supremal tolerances (maximal tolerances may not exist). Unfortunately, the suggested algorithm may, in the worst case, be exponential. Finally,Borges and Antunes (2002)present a geometric tolerance approach and develop accompanying visual interactive tools to deal with changes in the weights. Alternative approaches to sensitivity analysis in MOLP includeKornbluth (1974). This paper tests whether a specific efficient solution remains efficient upon a given change to the objective function coefficients, the right-hand-side and left-hand-side coefficients. It should be remarked that the paper only provides sufficient conditions for preserving efficiency. A number of papers use weight space decomposition in connection with sensitivity analysis of the objective function coefficients. These papers also determine changes to the objective function coefficients for a given efficient solution; see, for example,Yu and Zeleny (1976)andAntunes and ClГӯmaco (1992)for MOLPs andAlves and Costa (2016)for triobjective MILPs. The same applies toSitarz (2008,2010,2014). The main finding of these references is that for changes to a single coefficient, the sensitivity region is convex (but may not be closed). For changes to two coefficients,Sitarz (2010)demonstrates that the sensitivity region may neither be convex nor open or closed. We obtain similar convexity results. The objective function coefficients are also the focus ofBenson (1985). In addition to developing theoretical properties of the efficient set, the paper tests whether a given extreme point is efficient and provides conditions on the sensitivity region for a given extreme point. As indicated, most existing work gives conditions under which a specific efficient solution remains efficient upon change in the objective coefficients. On the contrary,Gal and Leberling (1981)give both necessary and sufficient conditions for the full efficient set to remain the same. This reference is the closest to our work. We likewise provide sufficient conditions for the efficient set to remain the same, although our sensitivity region may only be a subset of the changes for which the efficient set remains the same because of further structure assumptions. Our method, however, is specifically developed to handle discrete variables.Gal and Leberling (1981)rely on polyhedral theory and the Simplex method, and thus, their approach does not generalize to MOILP. More specifically, the authors exploit that efficient solutions are optimal solutions to weighted sum problems and determine the region of changes to the objective function coefficients and the right-hand sides for which the optimal bases of the weighted sum problems remain the same. In contrast, we do not rely on polyhedral properties of the feasible set but only exploit the linearity of the objective functions. 1.2. Overview and Contributions We emphasize that very few existing papers account for integrality. To the best of our knowledge, we are among the first to deal with the inclusion of discrete variables. In defining the sensitivity region, we require that the efficient set is the same upon the change in objective function coefficients. To address convexity issues, we further impose assumptions on the structure to be unchanged. In particular, we require that the component-wise relation between efficient solutions is preserved and that inefficient solutions remain inefficient, and we show how this ensures the preservation of the efficient set. Moreover, the structure assumption facilitates the development of a practical algorithm for computing this region. For changes to a single objective function coefficient, we first determine bounds on the sensitivity region set by the efficient solutions. We proceed to show that a subset of the inefficient solutions can be excluded from consideration and determine the bounds on the sensitivity region set by the remaining inefficient solutions. In fact, we prove that it suffices to inspect the inefficient solutions of aq-objective problem that correspond to efficient solutions of one of two relatedq+вҖү1-objective problems. Finally, we show that the sensitivity region is a convex set, that is, an interval. The restriction to inefficient solutions corresponding to efficient solutions of certain related multiobjective problems generalizes to simultaneous changes in two or more objective function coefficients. However, the computational experiments show that the sensitivity region may be nonconvex. The approach to sensitivity analysis in this paper applies to MOILPs, including binary problems. Our approach is independent of the solution method used for multiobjective programming and can, in principle, work with any algorithm and implementation. Unfortunately, existing methods to determine the full set of efficient solutions to a MOILP are limited. The branch-and-bound approach ofForget et al. (2022)is an example that operates in decision space. Nevertheless, we show that it is possible to apply algorithms that operate in objective space and are relatively fast. To provide preliminary computational experience and evidence of practical applicability of our method, we carry out numerical experiments for variations in one or more objective function coefficient(s) of the multiobjective knapsack problem, using the software developed byKlamroth et al. (2015)and implemented byDГӨchert et al. (2024). The potential of our method is further supported by the considerable progress during the last decade in generating efficient solutions and nondominated points of multiobjective integer programming problems. Examples include the papers byLokman and KГ¶ksalan (2013),Kirlik and Sayin (2014), andBГ¶kler and Mutzel (2015)that develop algorithms for obtaining all nondominated points to discrete and combinatorial multiobjective optimization problems. Also, available software packages can determine the set of nondominated points of MOILPs; see, for instance,Lokman and KГ¶ksalan (2013)andGandibleux et al. (2017a,b). We believe that our algorithm for sensitivity analysis can immediately be incorporated into future software packages and thereby be available to both the academic community and practitioners dealing with multiobjective optimization problems. The paper is structured as follows.Section 2gives a brief introduction to multiobjective programming. InSection 3, we discuss sensitivity of MOILPs with respect to changes in the objective function coefficients and define the sensitivity region. InSection 4, we consider variations in a single objective function coefficient, and inSection 5, we extend our analysis to simultaneous changes in multiple objective function coefficients.Section 6provides preliminary experimental results, andSection 7summarizes our findings.

The algorithm is able to determine all nondominated points for integer problems with an arbitrary number of objective functions. 6.1. Sensitivity Analysis on Capital Budgeting Problems We consider the capital budgeting problem byRosenblatt and Sinuany-Stern (1989), formulated as a variant of a biobjective binary knapsack problem. A number of potential investment projects can either be accepted or rejected. The aim is to invest in a portfolio of projects such that the total expected net present value of the portfolio is maximized, whereas the total portfolio risk, measured by its variance, is minimized. With independence between the projects, the objectives consist of a sum of net present values and a sum of variances, respectively. The investment is subject to a given budget on the initial expenditure. We denote the number of projects bynand characterize each project by its expected net present valueрқңҮрқ‘—Ојj, its varianceрқңҺ2рқ‘—Пғj2, and its first-period cash outflowрқ‘Һрқ‘—,рқ‘—=1,2,вҖҰ,рқ‘ӣaj,j=1,2,вҖҰ,n. article.7. Conclusion: This paper studies sensitivity analysis of the objective function coefficients in MultiObjective Integer Linear Programming. In particular, we show how to obtain the sensitivity region of permissible changes to the coefficients. The region is maximal in the sense that if a change belongs to the sensitivity region, the set of efficient solutions remains the same, and so does the component-wise relation between the efficient solutions; if a change does not belong to the sensitivity region, either the set of efficient solutions or the relation between them changes. To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to compute sensitivity regions for general MOILPs and the first to present extensive computational results. We illustrate the validity of our approach for a mean-variance portfolio problem of capital budgeting. The sensitivity regions can provide insights to managers regarding the stability of portfolios with respect to expected net present value and risk, irrespective of preferences. Furthermore, we do a number of experiments for multiobjective binary and general integer knapsack problems, the results of which demonstrate the potential of our approach for general MOILPs. Within less than half an hour of CPU time, we obtain all sensitivity regions for changes to one and two coefficients of biobjective problems with up to 500 binary variables or up to 100 integer variables.
